NorthBridge Bank — Module 1, Lesson 2
When Systems Fail — Understanding Service Dependencies
Select a numbered circle to explore each concept
1 INFRA­STRUCTURE
2 FIREWALL
3 LOAD BALANCER
4 DEPENDEN­CIES
5 EVIDENCE
The NorthBridge Infrastructure
In this lesson we expand the NorthBridge chain to 7 components, learn what the Firewall and Load Balancer do, and discover what happens when one component in the chain fails. Select a numbered circle above — or tap Start to begin.
Concept 1 of 5
The Full Infrastructure Chain
In Lesson 1 you learned five components. In a real banking environment,
two more components sit between the Internet and the Web Server.
🏠 NorthBridge — The Complete Chain
Customer Devices  →  Internet  →  Firewall  →  Load Balancer
 →  Web Servers (x3)  →  Network Switch  →  App Server  →  Database

Every request Rahul sends must pass through all components in sequence.
Which means we now have more potential failure points.

The two new components are the Firewall and the Load Balancer.
Let us understand each one.
Concept 2 of 5
The Firewall
The Firewall is a security boundary. Its only job is to look at every incoming connection and make one decision: allow it, or block it.
🏠 NorthBridge — The Security Gate
Think of the Firewall like the security checkpoint at the entrance of a large corporate building.

Every visitor must pass through security before entering.
If you have valid authorisation — you go through.
If you do not — you are turned away at the door.

The Firewall can detect and block:
  ▪  Suspicious connections — unusual patterns from unknown sources
  ▪  Unauthorised traffic — requests trying to reach restricted systems
  ▪  Known malicious activity — requests matching attack signatures

If the Firewall fails: either all traffic is blocked or the security boundary disappears entirely.
🔒 Ownership — The Firewall
Owned by: NorthBridge Network Security team. Sits between the Internet and the internal network. Any change to its configuration is treated as a high-risk engineering event.
Concept 3 of 5
The Load Balancer
The Load Balancer sits after the Firewall and distributes incoming requests across multiple Web Servers so no single server is overwhelmed.
🏠 NorthBridge — The Traffic Director
Think of a supermarket on a busy Monday morning.
If every customer went to the same checkout counter, the queue would stretch to the back of the store.

So the manager opens multiple counters and directs customers to whichever has the shortest queue.
That is exactly what a Load Balancer does.

It sits in front of multiple Web Servers.
Each incoming request is sent to whichever server is currently handling the least load.

If one Web Server becomes slow or unavailable,
the Load Balancer routes traffic to the remaining servers automatically.

If the Load Balancer itself fails: all traffic has nowhere to go — even if every other component is healthy.
🔒 Ownership — The Load Balancer
Owned by: NorthBridge Infrastructure team. Sits between the Firewall and the Web Servers. Its failure causes complete service outage even when all Web Servers are healthy.
Concept 4 of 5
Service Dependencies
A service dependency means: one system relies on another to function. If the system it depends on fails, it cannot complete its work — even if it itself is perfectly healthy.
🏠 NorthBridge — Failure Propagation
Suppose the Customer Database goes offline.

  Step 1: Database stops responding.
  Step 2: App Server gets no reply — cannot verify Rahul's credentials.
  Step 3: App Server returns an error to the Web Server.
  Step 4: Web Server returns that error to Rahul.
  Step 5: Rahul sees — Login Failed. Please try again later.

The visible failure is at Step 5.
The root cause is at Step 1.

Four components involved. Only one has actually failed.
Concept 5 of 5
Reading the Evidence
Engineers do not start by checking everything. They start by reading what is working — because everything working tells them exactly where the failure is not.
🏠 NorthBridge — Monday Morning Investigation
The website loads.  ✓  Internet path working. Firewall passing traffic. Load Balancer routing. Web Servers responding.

Four components — eliminated without touching a single server.

Login fails.  ✗  Something is wrong at the App Server or Database.

Seven components. Narrowed to two suspects — just by reading what is already working.

The error a customer sees tells you where the failure surfaced.
It does not tell you where it started.

Always trace the chain.
Concept 1 of 5
Key Principle
